summary:
The Arab Siege of Constantinople (674–678 AD) was the first major attempt by the [[event:2129|Umayyad Caliphate]] to capture the Byzantine capital, representing the high-water mark of early Islamic westward expansion and a crucial test of whether Christian Europe's most important city could withstand Islamic military pressure. The Umayyad caliph Muawiya I, having consolidated control over the vast Arab empire, launched a sustained naval and land campaign that blockaded Constantinople for four consecutive years — an unprecedented investment of military resources.

The siege failed decisively, and its failure was due in significant part to the Byzantine secret weapon known as [[event:2205|Greek fire]] — an incendiary liquid projected through siphons onto enemy ships that could burn on water and was extraordinarily difficult to extinguish. Arab naval forces attempting to blockade the sea walls suffered repeated devastating losses to Greek fire attacks, and their fleet was severely damaged in multiple engagements. The combination of Constantinople's massive Theodosian walls, Greek fire, and a storm that wrecked the retreating Arab fleet on the return voyage inflicted such severe losses that the Umayyads agreed to a thirty-year peace treaty and paid annual tribute to Byzantium. The failed siege demonstrated that Constantinople was effectively impregnable to contemporary military technology, preserving the Byzantine Empire for another eight centuries (until 1453) and blocking the Islamic expansion into southeastern Europe that would have fundamentally altered the continent's religious and cultural development.
